8: hagios hagios hagios Kyrios ho Theos ho Pantokrator ho en kai ho on kai ho erchomenos: 'Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is to come.' The Trisagion (Isa 6:3) is adapted with the temporal formula that characterizes Revelation's divine self-identification. The fourfold repetition of praise from the four living creatures links Revelation's throne-room to Isaiah's call-vision: the same divine holiness that destroyed Isaiah's self-confidence before the vision of YHWH is now the content of ceaseless heavenly worship. John hears the cosmic liturgy that underlies all earthly worship.
1: idou thyra ēnoigmenē en tō ouranō: 'Behold, a door was standing open in heaven.' Ēnoigmenē (perfect passive participle): the door had been opened and stands open — not freshly opened at the moment of John's vision but already-open for his entry. Anaba hōde kai deixō soi ha dei genesthai meta tauta: come up here and I will show you what must take place after these things — dei genesthai (must happen) repeats the divine necessity of 1:1. The ascent structure follows Jewish apocalyptic convention (1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, Isa 6) — the seer is taken up to receive the heavenly perspective on earthly events.
2: euthēōs egenomēn en pneumati: 'immediately I was in the Spirit.' The second Spirit-transport in Revelation (cf. 1:10) — egenomēn en pneumati (aorist: I came to be in the Spirit) marks a definite transition. Kai idou thronos ekeito en tō ouranō, kai epi ton thronon kathēmenos: the throne as the visual center of the vision — who sits on it is not immediately named but described in terms of precious-stone appearance, deliberately avoiding direct description of the divine form (as in Ezek 1:26-28, which speaks of 'something like' human form on the throne).
3: ho kathēmenos homoios horasei lithō iaspidi kai sardios, kai iris kyklothen tou thronou homoios horasei smaragdinō: 'the one seated had the appearance of jasper and carnelian, and around the throne was a rainbow that had the appearance of an emerald.' The description proceeds entirely through simile (homoios: like, resembling) — the divine form is never described directly, only through layers of comparison. Iaspis (jasper): translucent crystal (21:11 calls it 'clear as crystal'); sardios (carnelian): deep red. The rainbow (iris) of emerald green alludes to Ezek 1:28 (the rainbow around the divine glory) — God's covenant promise encircling his throne.
4: kyklothen tou thronou thronoi eikosi tessares, kai epi tous thronous eikosi tessaras presbyterous kathēmenous: 'Around the throne were twenty-four thrones, and seated on the thrones were twenty-four elders.' Eikosi tessaras presbyterous: the identity of the twenty-four elders is debated. Three main proposals: (1) the 24 Davidic priestly courses of 1 Chr 24:1-19; (2) the 12 patriarchs + 12 apostles (the whole covenant people); (3) an angelic council. Their white garments and golden stephanoi (victor's wreaths, not royal crowns) favor a redeemed-humanity reading. The position of sitting on thrones signals delegated authority.
5: kai ek tou thronou ekporeuontai astrapai kai phōnai kai brontai: 'From the throne come flashes of lightning and rumblings and peals of thunder.' The three-element theophany formula (lightning, voices, thunder) recurs at each major structural marker in Revelation (8:5; 11:19; 16:18), always with escalating intensity — this is the Sinai-theophany pattern (Exod 19:16) transposed to the heavenly throne. Hepta lampades pyros kaiomenai enōpion tou thronou, hai eisin ta hepta pneumata tou theou: the seven torches are identified as the seven Spirits of God — the same Spirit-fullness present in the churches (1:4; 3:1) is here a blazing heavenly presence before the throne.
6: kai enōpion tou thronou hōs thalassa hyalinē homoion krystallō: 'Before the throne was something like a sea of glass, like crystal.' The glassy sea combines the bronze sea of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 7:23-26, the large basin for priestly purification) and the sapphire pavement of Exod 24:10 (where the elders saw God at Sinai). Tessara zōia gemonta ophthalmon emprosthen kai opisthen: four living creatures full of eyes in front and behind — omniscient, all-seeing, combining Ezekiel's four creatures (Ezek 1:5-14) with Isaiah's seraphim (Isa 6:2-3).
7: to zōon to prōton homoion leonti, kai to deuteron zōon homoion moschō, kai to triton zōon echon to prosōpon hōs anthrōpou, kai to tetarton zōon homoion aetō petomenō: the four faces — lion, ox (or calf: moschos), human, eagle — correspond to Ezek 1:10's four faces of the heavenly creatures. In patristic tradition these four were linked to the four Evangelists (Irenaeus; Jerome). More precisely, they represent the four royal/divine qualities: lion (king of beasts, power), ox (service, sacrifice), human (intelligence, reason), eagle (divinity, transcendence, heaven).
9: kai hotan dōsousin ta zōia doxan kai timēn kai eucharistian tō kathēmenō epi tō thronō: 'And whenever the living creatures give glory and honor and thanksgiving to him who is seated on the throne.' The temporal conjunction hotan (whenever) + future indicatives describes an ongoing, repeated liturgical cycle — the heavenly worship is continual. The three-fold doxa, timē, eucharistia (glory, honor, thanksgiving) forms a liturgical pattern that the twenty-four elders' response (v. 10) expands.
10: pesountai hoi eikosi tessares presbyteroi enōpion tou kathēmenou epi tou thronou kai proskyneusousin tō zōnti eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn kai balousin tous stephanous autōn enōpion tou thronou: 'The twenty-four elders will fall down before him who is seated on the throne and worship him who lives forever and ever, and they cast their crowns before the throne.' The casting of stephanoi (crowns/wreaths) before the throne is the decisive gesture: the elders' authority and victory belongs to God, not to themselves. The victor's wreath has no meaning except as returned to its source.
11: axios ei, ho kyrios kai ho theos hēmōn, labein tēn doxan kai tēn timēn kai tēn dynamin: 'Worthy are you, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power.' Axios (worthy) is the key term of chs. 4-5: first applied to God in the creation-doxology, then contested in ch. 5 (who is worthy to open the scroll?), then applied to the Lamb (5:9, 12). Ho kyrios kai ho theos hēmōn: 'our Lord and God' — this is precisely the title that the Emperor Domitian demanded for himself (Dominus et Deus noster); the throne-room doxology directly counters the imperial claim.
